channel.description: A growing archive of Alan Watts talks, enriched with transcript-derived summaries, chapters, and episode artwork.
channel.itunes:author: Alan Watts

item.title: Seeing Security Everywhere
item.description:
This talk begins with a personal preamble about returning from retreat, grief, caretaking, and the difficulty of speaking from a place that still feels in process. From there, the speaker turns to a sutta on fear, strife, and the wish for a haven, using it to trace a path from ethical conduct to concentration, insight, and the steady release of grasping. The talk keeps circling back to the same point in different forms: practice is not a linear ascent, but an interwoven training in how the world conditions the mind and how the mind learns to respond without clinging. Near the end, the focus shifts to the ordinary sense doors, the problem of “me and mine,” and the possibility of seeing security without relying on abstraction.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening in Retreat 06:50 What the Sutta Asks 09:07 Ethics and Cultivation 20:29 Fear and Competition 24:25 Training for Unbinding 31:26 Attention and Jhana 38:32 Conceit and the Raft 44:52 Truth Made Concrete 53:26 No Mine, No Grasping
Source: Google Drive Uploads
Original page: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1DjwsTgws1fDB-rcdfb6rgQcBWPaiktno/view

item.title: Limits of Language
item.description:
This talk begins by taking aim at a familiar Western habit: treating the world as if it were made of inert stuff shaped by an outside force. From that opening, the speaker moves through language, science, and comparative ideas from Sanskrit, Chinese thought, Buddhism, and Christianity to argue that what we call matter is really form, pattern, and process. The second half turns from theory to practice, pressing the point that spiritual life is not a future reward but an immediate shift in attention, with yoga, meditation, and the Sermon on the Mount all used to unsettle delay, worry, and ego.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the head trip 01:31 Form and matter 03:17 Language makes ghosts 05:30 Self-shaping nature 09:49 The knower and mystery 15:37 Named form and pattern 22:07 Suchness and postponement 32:05 Yoga and silence 39:42 Now, not the ego
Source: Google Drive Uploads
Original page: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ZVasVfCsIK-Zj-r1tsQizw0uS7ed0owN/view

item.title: Buddhism Explained
item.description:
This talk explains Buddhism as a method rather than a creed, starting with Sanskrit terms, Buddhist cosmology, and the figure of the Buddha as one who is awake. It then moves through the Four Noble Truths and the trap of trying to defeat desire, using repeated dialogue between teacher and student to show how spiritual effort can become another form of striving. Near the end, the talk widens into a broader argument about responsibility, spontaneity, and why both Buddhist and Christian sayings about non-anxiety are so hard to live by. The closing turn is stark: nirvana is presented not as a solution, but as giving up the search for one.
Chapters: 00:00 Word Origins 01:55 Buddhism as Method 03:24 The Wheel of Life 09:12 The Still Center 13:28 Solitude and Renunciation 20:40 The Middle Way 23:53 Four Noble Truths 33:10 The Frustration Trap 41:28 Anxiety and Release
Source: Google Drive Uploads
Original page: https://drive.google.com/file/d/15FPUeBZLxaNER1RpYnaW2iijwGI4wiSc/view

item.title: Silence & Reality
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple provocation: before trying to save or destroy the planet, pause in real silence and stop treating thought as reality. From there it moves through money, ego, death, and time, arguing that many of the things people take as solid are only concepts that stand in for immediate experience. In the middle, the speaker turns to meditation, Zen, and music as ways of entering a non-instrumental present; later the talk becomes more technical, taking on causation, grammar, and the idea of a separate will. A short question period at the end checks how this view fits with ordinary functioning, and the answer is that meditation need not erase practical life to loosen the grip of these ideas.
Chapters: 00:01 A Moment of Silence 02:15 Reality Beyond Concepts 04:32 Ego, Death, and Time 07:02 Passing the Buck 09:58 Zen and Stopping Thought 12:37 No Future in Meditation 17:25 Sound, Meaning, and Participation 21:43 One Organism in Motion 30:17 Grammar, Will, and Process
Source: Google Drive Uploads
Original page: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1fCXR4PTUc6QCuhUeQeM-Z2pH8GiLB9CF/view

item.title: Hinduism Fully Explained
item.description:
The talk begins with the Upanishads and Vedanta, laying out the claim that the Self is Brahman and that the world’s multiplicity is shaped by maya. From there it turns to the hard problem of non-duality: why self and other, one and many, can only be described through the very oppositions they seem to escape. The second half widens into social order, using caste, vocation, and life stages to show how Hinduism links metaphysics to everyday conduct before returning to the larger image of the world as play and drama. The closing stretch circles back to Vedanta’s distinction between the manifest stage and the unmanifest green room, ending on the question of whether the self is a fixed identity or a role being acted.
Chapters: 00:14 Upanishads and Vedanta 02:59 Brahman and maya 08:08 The logic of opposites 14:11 No-thing and the stage 25:14 Hindu life and shaman 29:19 Caste and life stages 41:21 Vocation and renunciation 01:06:11 Atman is Brahman 01:17:06 Hide-and-seek and the play
Source: Google Drive Uploads
Original page: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1yjOLl8EUwIUtwm6tm_GRCJ2bkk-E8qUQ/view

item.title: Close Your Eyes
item.description:
The talk opens by contrasting Western religions as largely verbal with Asian contemplative practice, then uses that contrast to ask why meditation has become attractive to modern listeners. From there it moves through a critique of forced discipline and ego-striving, into a more practical account of wu-wei, silence, and the way symbols get mistaken for reality. The second half becomes increasingly instructional: listening, breathing, chanting, and the use of sound are presented as ways to settle the mind and experience the present directly.
Chapters: 00:00 Why Meditation Attracts 04:17 Alienation And Discipline 09:19 Forcing Backfires 14:10 Space And The Child Mind 19:52 Return To Direct Experience 27:50 Reality Beyond Symbols 34:15 Tools For Listening 40:47 Breath Without Forcing 47:44 Mantra And Closing Practice
Source: Google Drive Uploads
Original page: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1J8nTjOCLLlCrVdIyhgl6ehS2ZM5Zrw05/view

item.title: Not What Should Be But What Is
item.description:
The talk begins by questioning what the word “I” really points to, then builds a case that the ordinary ego is a mistaken image cut off from the larger world that produces it. From there it moves through environmental crisis, the limits of conscious thought, and the failure of self-improvement schemes, using music, psychology, religion, Zen, and yoga to show why the self cannot fix itself by force. In the final stretch, the argument turns from critique to release: meditation is framed as a collapse of striving, a loosening of blame, and a shift into the sense that reality happens now rather than in a dead past.
Chapters: 00:00 The False I 01:45 Separate From Nature 05:09 Linear Mind, Vast World 10:10 Symbol Versus Reality 18:39 The Egoic Mask 26:01 Chronic Tension 30:47 No Method 40:03 Watching What Happens 48:18 Now and Responsibility
Source: Google Drive Uploads
Original page: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1gIh1LIn937P7S_u527mnJ3z22odPzGXB/view

item.title: How to Get Out of Your Own Way
item.description:
The talk begins by setting up a stubborn paradox: if the mind is selfish, how can it improve itself without already having changed? From Christian grace to Buddhist letting-go, it moves through a series of familiar spiritual solutions only to show how each can slip back into the same self-assertion it meant to defeat. The middle broadens into social and practical examples—medicine, genetics, politics, education—where good intentions and schemes of control create their own distortions. It ends by backing away from self-improvement altogether and asking whether the real task is simply to stop interfering and watch what is happening.
Chapters: 00:01 The mind-control problem 00:38 Grace and the vicious circle 02:42 Buddha and letting go 04:31 Why improvement loops 09:13 The higher self disguise 12:08 One-upmanship everywhere 16:58 Good intentions go wrong 26:09 Virtue without display 38:34 Stop getting in the way
Source: Google Drive Uploads
Original page: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1BY8mI3HJN7aF9EjN4FprwBA7Nqj4qk9s/view

item.title: Myth of Myself
item.description:
The talk opens by treating identity as the central mystery, then steadily argues against the familiar picture of the self as a little observer sealed inside the head. It moves from the cultural habit of feeling separate from the world, through the myths of a made universe and a blind mechanical cosmos, to a more unified view in which consciousness and environment belong together. A later section introduces the contrast between spotlight and floodlight awareness, before the talk returns to death, guilt, and the social habits that keep people feeling like strangers in existence.
Chapters: 00:01 Who Am I? 01:17 The Skin-Ego 05:24 Alienation From Nature 08:59 Myths of Making 13:42 God and Mechanism 19:07 Intelligence in Nature 24:11 Science and the Whole 30:04 Spotlight and Floodlight 35:48 Belonging to the Earth
Source: YouTube / Being in the Way
Original page: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6rlrz1bcups

item.title: In the Spirit with Alan Watts
item.description:
The talk opens by arguing that what Western thought calls “nothing” is not an absence but the ground that makes experience possible, using Aquinas and a comic travel story to turn the question of spiritual achievement inside out. It then moves through a series of Zen explanations and vivid Japanese scenes—rain, tea, ink, calligraphy—to show practice as direct, unforced attention rather than doctrine. From there, the talk sharpens into a meditation on emptiness, the self, and the Heart Sutra’s claim that form and void are inseparable, before ending on the claim that ordinary sounds and sights need no translation at all.
Chapters: 00:01 Nothing as the ground 00:40 Why you can't start here 01:47 Insight beyond words 03:10 The long silence 05:02 Japan in rain and tea 09:22 Watching without comment 10:36 Emptiness and form 13:14 Ordinary things, Zen 14:11 Listening for the untranslatable
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=14Zd7qZbTVb1ywWyOaIkmsJpWfATyKRdS

item.title: In the Spirit with Alan Watts
item.description:
The talk opens as a reading from a newly published autobiography, then quickly turns into a self-portrait built around paradox: the speaker as fool, mystic, rascal, and unsteady seeker. From there it moves through Zen, Taoism, and Catholic ritual, using stories and quotations to argue that insight comes less from effort or doctrine than from letting thought fall quiet. In the later stretch, the conversation widens to psychiatry, universities, politics, LSD, and social change, but keeps returning to the same insistence that direct experience matters more than spiritual posturing.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the autobiography 00:52 Comic self-portrait 03:16 Shaman, priest, wanderer 08:44 Many traditions, one fascination 12:57 Letting concepts fall 16:51 Zen without the pose 19:32 Silence, sound, and ritual 26:31 Krishnamurti and laughter 30:40 Religion and the non-ego
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1p4H8hPa7FK4-mZCtK6NNo3YZ0Ox5eehK

item.title: A Memorial Tribute to Alan Watts
item.description:
A group of friends gathers for a memorial tribute that moves between recollection, playful anecdote, and ritual remembrance. The talk sketches the speaker’s life through stories of his early broadcasting, his books, his friendships, and the distinct blend of Zen, wit, and improvisation that shaped his public voice. It also turns to the funeral itself, described in Zen Buddhist terms, before closing with a reading on the unborn, indestructible self.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the memorial 02:38 Who Watts was 06:14 Cremation and return 12:19 Friends remember 16:20 Play, logic, and voice 21:42 Presence on air 27:48 Showman and listener 36:56 Generosity and art 44:23 Funeral, influence, legacy
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1LTs-yBE-m8ZqKsKzMO5aJSTkL_8mz1yo

item.title: Man is a Hoax
item.description:
The talk opens with a critique of how children are treated as if they are not yet fully human, then widens into a broad attack on education, work, success, and retirement as systems that keep people living for an always-deferred future. From there it turns to the machinery of identity: the social mask of the “person,” the split between voluntary and involuntary life, and the sense of alienation that follows when the self is imagined as separate from the world. In its later movement, the speaker brings in breathing, Genesis, Big Bang imagery, and Zen-style questioning to argue that responsibility and individuality are usually misunderstood, and that the modern myth of a mechanically ordered universe has quietly taken hold.
Chapters: 00:00 Children on Probation 01:07 The Preparation Machine 03:51 Work, Money, Pleasure 06:19 Living in Tomorrow 09:24 Person as Mask 13:08 Split Self, Split World 17:31 Breath and Inseparability 21:24 Blame and Cosmic Action 26:23 Who Are You, Really?
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1vrH5FMgf8qcukNnex2l96B7ISwgluFJn

item.title: The Symbolic and The Real
item.description:
This talk moves from a simple contrast—money and wealth, words and things—into a larger argument about how symbolic thought shapes human consciousness. From there it turns to self-awareness, time, anxiety, and the mythic idea of the fall, using examples from the Rorschach test, a circle on the blackboard, and the relation between figure and background to show how attention isolates what it notices. In the final stretch, the speaker widens the frame again: the individual is not a sealed ego but a local expression of a larger whole, and the practical problem is learning to keep analysis and total awareness in balance.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the problem 01:02 Money and words 02:51 What symbols do 07:20 Time, death, and anxiety 14:33 Consciousness as spotlight 20:30 Rorschach and the circle 28:45 Space is not nothing 31:38 The cosmos in focus 37:32 Leaves, not visitors
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=17xcLyneiUbg0BufT8Nsrb3HyKTeb3zdl

item.title: Mysticism and Morals
item.description:
The talk begins by defining mysticism not as doctrine but as a sudden state of consciousness: a feeling that the self and the world are one, and that everything belongs in a harmonious order. From there, it moves into a sharp critique of forced spirituality and then turns to morals, arguing that ethical life is less about outward compliance than about honest feeling, with religion’s priestly impulse often at odds with that inward truth. The talk closes by tying the two together: mystic vision sees the divinity of whatever is, while moral seriousness depends on admitting what one actually feels without pretending.
Chapters: 00:01 Defining Mysticism 04:54 False Harmony 11:12 Religion as Play 20:16 Morals as Rules 25:24 Mystics and Priests 29:08 Inward Law 36:53 Honesty in Practice 47:13 Feeling Without Pretence 56:41 Final Synthesis
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=13PiZ_gbemrQ8mjKgogj_gEozPCTscRGK
item.itunes:author: Alan Watts
item.itunes:summary:
The talk begins by defining mysticism not as doctrine but as a sudden state of consciousness: a feeling that the self and the world are one, and that everything belongs in a harmonious order. From there, it moves into a sharp critique of forced spirituality and then turns to morals, arguing that ethical life is less about outward compliance than about honest feeling, with religion’s priestly impulse often at odds with that inward truth. The talk closes by tying the two together: mystic vision sees the divinity of whatever is, while moral seriousness depends on admitting what one actually feels without pretending.
Chapters: 00:01 Defining Mysticism 04:54 False Harmony 11:12 Religion as Play 20:16 Morals as Rules 25:24 Mystics and Priests 29:08 Inward Law 36:53 Honesty in Practice 47:13 Feeling Without Pretence 56:41 Final Synthesis
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=13PiZ_gbemrQ8mjKgogj_gEozPCTscRGK

item.title: The Tea Ceremony
item.description:
The talk begins by separating the modern tea ceremony from its older Zen roots, then follows the Bodhidharma legend into a larger account of Buddhism as a practice of awakening from delusion. From there it widens into a discussion of Confucian reverence, the social formalities of ordinary life, and the way the tea ceremony gathers gardening, ceramics, architecture, painting, and poetry into one disciplined art. The final stretch turns to two stories—one about scattered leaves in a garden, another about a broken tea caddy repaired with gold—to argue that the deepest beauty in this tradition comes from naturalness, chance, and even mistaken action.
Chapters: 00:01 Tea Beyond Refinement 02:50 Bodhidharma and Wakefulness 05:23 Ritual in Daily Life 09:57 A Synthesis of Arts 12:06 Entering the Tea Garden 15:36 Nature, Not Sterility 18:02 Cup, Whisk, and Texture 21:59 Mistakes as Beauty
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1-POJq8TEKlNQzjvugAS3isD9-i6MeaP_

item.title: Tribute to C. G. Jung
item.description:
The talk begins as a personal tribute to Carl Jung, recalled from a shelf of books and from a meeting in 1958, then turns to why Jung mattered as a corrective to one-sided enthusiasms for Eastern thought. From there it develops into a sustained argument about polarity, projection, and the danger of treating evil as an absolute outside oneself, with Adolf Eichmann used as a vivid test case. The middle and late sections move into Jung’s own words on accepting the dark side, healing, egoism, and the uneasy way Christian language, Eastern philosophy, and psychology can converge around self-knowledge.
Chapters: 00:01 A late-night tribute 00:58 East and West 02:45 Polarity of life 05:00 Projection and violence 08:14 The accepted dark side 13:38 Reading Jung’s passage 20:47 Healing and egoism 24:16 Enantiodromia and unity 28:16 Archetypes in art
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1TSnTQMvcHfWJ1t1Ch15WSUoHD4uUU9pq

item.title: Astrology
item.description:
The talk begins with a listener’s enthusiastic defense of astrology and uses that as a way into a careful, skeptical exploration of why the subject can seem convincing. It weighs symbolic and psychological readings of astrology, especially through Jung, before shifting to the limits of prediction, the problem of self-fulfilling expectations, and a failed attempt to test charts against medical cases. In the final stretch, the speaker broadens the argument to Tao, critical openness, and the warning not to confuse mystical unity with control over events, ending on the image of the world as improvisation rather than a fixed score.
Chapters: 00:01 The listener's challenge 01:51 Why astrology seems plausible 07:11 Jung and symbolic rhythm 11:39 Patterns, not proofs 16:23 Prediction and disappointment 19:29 War and postmortems 21:52 A critical openness 23:39 Mysticism is not occult power 27:31 Improvisation
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1M2B0tP0MrF61FhhUaOHHKMSAqcGnl-Ly

item.title: Haiku
item.description:
The talk begins with several haiku read aloud, then unfolds into an argument for why the form feels both spare and exacting. From there, the speaker moves through Zen ideas of direct seeing, ordinary action, and the value of stopping before explanation takes over, using brief stories and many poems to make the point. Midway through, the discussion turns more concrete: translation, formal limits, seasonal subjects, and examples from Basho and other poets show how haiku works by suggestion rather than statement. It closes by widening the idea to painting and to Zen sayings, ending on the thought that ordinary perception itself can be the path.
Chapters: 00:01 What Haiku Does 01:59 Zen as Source 06:10 Ordinary Answers 10:39 Moments of Perception 13:24 Basho and No-Mind 16:27 Form and Restraint 19:25 The Reader's Share 24:12 Thusness and Closing Examples
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=15Ppcv3fYgS4d9_6g1oPjgjgkO7JfEWkW

item.title: Answering Questions of Requests from Listeners
item.description:
The talk begins as a reply to listener questions, first taking up the charge that Eastern religion lacks humanitarian concern and then pushing back with examples of ahimsa, Buddhist literature, and Ashoka’s social works. It moves on to Martin Buber and Judaism, where the speaker separates cultural belonging from religious stereotype and argues that fixed labels can shrink freedom and humanity. In the second half, the focus shifts to larger claims about authority, fear, motivation, and the relation between a person and environment, ending with a brief, dry answer to a question about criticism and puritanical young women.
Chapters: 00:01 Questions and assumptions 02:53 Eastern compassion 04:44 Christian charity and poverty 07:35 Buber and religious labels 14:08 Hasidic stories 15:08 God as father 17:49 Fear and morality 22:00 Motivation and process 27:39 Criticism and perspective
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1F3pG0O7h5TKz8CrBnhNtn9WtN3dDabor

item.title: Philosophy As Music
item.description:
The talk begins by taking Gustav Holst’s claim that music is a universal language and using it to ask what it means for anything to communicate without stating propositions. From there it moves into a broader argument: music, philosophy, and even mystical experience can be understood as intelligible patterns that do not point beyond themselves, but disclose a kind of order in their own form. In the second half, the speaker turns to Chinese and Hindu thought to defend difference, conflict, and variety as part of a larger harmony rather than evidence of contradiction or failure.
Chapters: 00:30 Holst’s Opening Claim 01:00 Music Beyond Words 03:55 Nonverbal Communion 07:07 Philosophy as Pattern 10:41 Action Ends in Contemplation 13:23 Mystical Vision 15:39 Objections and Balance 22:43 Difference Makes Harmony
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1CR0S8CdA0uYQ4KZQygxZAdFjRV9DUxDa

item.title: Art, Philosophy and Self-Improvement
item.description:
The talk begins by pushing back against the idea that a teacher or philosopher ought to be morally complete, then turns to the claim that self-improvement always comes with tradeoffs and limits. From there it shifts into an argument for seeing art and Taoist philosophy as ways of apprehending life as a whole rather than as tools for moral correction, with Jung and Chuang Tzu used to sharpen that point. A brief comic story about monkeys and chestnuts leads into a final distinction between cynical exposure and a deeper acceptance of the dark side as part of any complete view of life.
Chapters: 00:00 Idols and ordinary lives 02:01 What improvement can mean 05:38 Jung and the divided self 10:53 Art versus exhortation 14:56 Life as a whole 18:49 Chuang Tzu's comic example 20:56 Against cynicism 24:00 Beyond defects and neuroses
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1Kp36YV9W7V2xNRW5rpYQwFbrWXQfuML7

item.title: Natural Law
item.description:
The talk opens by challenging the habit of reading Eastern thought through the Western idea of immutable natural law, with a sharp detour through Sir Edwin Arnold’s poetic version of the Buddha. From there it traces how ideas of law move from Indian and Chinese terms to Roman and Christian theology, then into the scientific picture of a mechanically ordered universe. In the last third, the speaker narrows the argument to epistemology and ethics, asking whether regularity is really a law in the world or a pattern our own structures help disclose.
Chapters: 00:00 Natural law and projection 02:19 Karma and dharma 04:10 Law in Christianity 05:32 The universe as code 07:17 From theism to deism 09:47 Why science prunes 14:02 Patterns, clocks, and rulers 18:09 Observer and world 21:00 Ethics without external rules
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1Ay2QJ4G80k4EG8BWjsRx9ptfpnQBDZmD

item.title: Bernard Beinson's War Diaries
item.description:
The talk opens with a passage from Bernard Berenson’s War Diaries and uses it to question the common suspicion of the first person: saying “I,” the speaker argues, is not the same as vanity. From there it widens into a brisk meditation on ego, self-consciousness, and why efforts at humility can turn into concealment, with detours through Chesterton, Jane Austen, and a wry comment about spiritual teachers who still look very human. The second half pushes the argument further, contrasting Western moral judgment with Eastern contemplative aims, and ending with a defense of full participation in life rather than aloofness from it.
Chapters: 00:00 Berenson and the word I 00:48 False humility 03:22 The hidden ego 04:22 Ego and spiritual pose 06:15 West meets East 08:23 Not a superhuman ideal 10:15 Sensitivity versus self-deception 13:18 The defensive ego 16:55 Trusting life
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1L1rjJqlHtJEw7doPDMEtmhN0kEj1Pfok

item.title: Karma, Law of Retribution
item.description:
The talk begins by revisiting karma in Indian philosophy and argues that its usual reading as a law of retribution is too literal. From there, it shifts into a critique of cause and effect itself, using examples from hunger, death, falling stones, and sunflowers to suggest that necessity often belongs to our definitions and descriptions, not to nature as such. Later, the speaker brings in Wittgenstein and a grid or pattern analogy to show how language and thought impose regularities on the world, and how that can create the illusion of fixed laws. The result is less a defense of karma than a broader attempt to loosen the grip of metaphysical certainty.
Chapters: 00:30 Opening the problem 02:26 Karma and retribution 05:30 Seeing through cosmology 10:07 Action versus necessity 13:43 Food, hunger, death 18:30 Rocks, sunflowers, and must 24:15 Grids over nature
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1f80fYkhtoCs5LmuQBKLkdFOxNl1k1Nls

item.title: The Libido For Ugliness
item.description:
The talk opens with a reading from H. L. Mencken on the ugliness of industrial Pennsylvania, then turns to a local California example: the pressure to run a six-lane highway through Marin County’s coast and hills. From there it widens into a critique of “progress,” mass tourism, bureaucracy, and the habit of treating nature as something to conquer rather than live within. The closing movement links bad design to bad cooking and, more broadly, to a culture that seems to damage its surroundings and itself.
Chapters: 00:00 Mencken’s indictment 10:21 Marin under pressure 15:12 When beauty is consumed 18:11 Progress and machinery 20:51 Self-hatred in culture 23:02 Food and final warning
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1i9lryIDIu_i7qN-bzWP55Bj_Yd9yVGoq

item.title: The Constitution of Nature
item.description:
The talk sets out three historical ways of imagining nature: as a construct, as a drama, and as an organism. It starts with the Western habit of seeing the world as made from outside, moves through Indian and Chinese alternatives, and then weighs what each view makes possible or distorts. The final section turns practical, arguing that the mechanical outlook has helped science and technology but also left modern life fragmented, psychologically strained, and too dependent on specialization. It closes by looking toward a more organic, relational way of thinking that could better fit biology, ecology, and the world’s increasing complexity.
Chapters: 00:01 Three Views of Nature 01:55 Nature as Construct 05:27 Machine and Parts 08:08 The World as Drama 12:40 The Organic View 16:00 Costs of Mechanism 19:52 Specialization and Complexity 23:13 Toward a New Practical Philosophy
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1vPejIGWO19DdezNRLe-61S4hWFL9r7iA

item.title: Love of Waters
item.description:
This talk begins as a personal meditation on the sea, moving from sensory description to a larger claim that waves offer a kind of timeless time. It then turns sharper and more philosophical, using the gull, the crab, and the fish to argue that life is inseparable from eating, being eaten, and transformation. From there, the speaker questions human self-consciousness, contrasts isolated abstraction with living relation, and returns to the ocean as a place where the world feels joined rather than split apart. The closing image folds the whole meditation inward: the knocking at the edge of space turns out to be the speaker’s own heart.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening Love of the Sea 01:01 Timeless Waves 02:23 Sausalito from the Water 05:13 The Ocean’s Paradox 06:54 Crab, Gull, and Human Fear 09:47 Objecting to Life 12:05 Self-Consciousness and the Fall 15:22 Part and Whole 17:34 Back to the Heart
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1LLje9vCPhpOytlJeSnEoqEzl7IMfhPbW

item.title: Levels of Magnification
item.description:
The talk opens in the shadow of nuclear danger and the strange everyday indifference that can surround it. From there, the speaker builds a case for “active” acceptance: not resignation, but a wider way of seeing that can hold conflict and harmony at different levels at once. The argument moves through examples from physics and biology, then returns to human fear, identity, and the temptation to panic when the future feels unstable. It closes by asking for a larger sense of belonging that would make uncertainty bearable without forcing a false certainty.
Chapters: 00:00 Atomic-age alarm 02:43 Acceptance before change 04:56 Thinking on two levels 07:25 Cells and wholes 10:06 Race and cosmos 13:52 Belonging and meaning 17:02 Anxiety and escalation 20:25 A larger identity 25:27 Socratic coda
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1z7PcnIpc3h_ozG8UE7mIJ9oTxkb3Z4oY

item.title: The Study of Asia
item.description:
The talk opens with a brief thank-you to listeners for helping support Sabro Hasegawa, then turns quickly into a critique of how Asian studies are usually framed. From there, the speaker argues that the real value of studying Asia is not practical advice or antiquarian detail, but a way to expose the hidden assumptions of Western culture by comparison. The lecture moves through examples from Joseph Needham, Chinese science, and Japanese and Indian thought before ending on a defense of imagination, play, and Taoist non-doing as conditions for serious intellectual work.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening thanks 00:31 Why Asian studies began 01:50 Against narrow definitions 04:04 The Western crisis 06:52 Not a borrowed solution 10:15 Asia as contrast 12:46 Needham as model 15:37 Modern Asia, old questions 19:45 Play over utility
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1v8no8BiShvhiQJadWrlUKLRVvspfaYxp

item.title: The Fourth Way
item.description:
The talk begins as a response to a listener’s request for further comments on Ouspensky’s The Fourth Way, then widens into a comparison of Gurdjieff, Zen, and Taoist teaching. From there it argues that real instruction often works by obstruction, misdirection, and the frustration of fixed ideas rather than by clear explanation. The closing movement ties that method to Buddhist upaya, or skillful means, and ends by recasting spiritual seeking as a problem of wanting to get something in the first place.
Chapters: 00:00 The Listener’s Request 02:33 Zen as Barrier 05:32 Words and Skill 08:33 Gurdjieff’s School 13:16 Seeing Through the Game 19:20 The Sly Way 22:06 Shocking the Student 23:25 Skillful Means
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=18maZhLLVDhCQe87cbjKuVdbIrvP8xX64

item.title: Summary Of Recent Programs
item.description:
The talk opens by taking the saying “by their fruits ye shall know them” as a test of philosophy, then quickly turns that standard around to question whether survival is really the highest moral value. From there, it builds a critique of childhood self-control, Freudian splits between mind and body, and the habit of treating discipline as something opposed to spontaneity. The second half broadens the argument through the Buddhist raft image, a discussion of Asian liberating traditions, and a contrast between utilitarian survival and a more musical, playful view of life. It closes by arguing that real liberation does not preach at the world but restores it through delight.
Chapters: 00:30 Fruits and moral test 04:13 Childhood and control 07:08 Alienation from spontaneity 10:53 The raft must be left 15:15 Adult liberation 18:22 Utility versus delight 21:38 Music as a model 24:38 Rhythm, play, Orpheus
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1Ofv-joWEQDvWqRgvGVIquoyYmtAJwn2i

item.title: Varieties of Order
item.description:
The talk starts with a familiar philosophical problem: whether societies should be understood as organisms or as loose collections of individuals. From there, it moves through nominalism and realism, then turns to the limits of committees, organizations, and rule-based ethics, arguing that living intelligence is subtler than mechanical order. The later sections widen the frame again, bringing in Confucius, a Chuang Tzu wheelwright story, and finally Chinese ideas of Li to distinguish social machinery from a deeper, more complex order of nature.
Chapters: 00:21 Society as Organism? 01:21 Individuals and Collectives 04:33 Communication Creates Groups 06:47 The Limits of Social Machinery 10:13 Against Rule Worship 12:48 Confucius and the Wheelwright 16:49 Small Teams, Then God 21:12 Li and Hidden Pattern 25:08 Beyond Individuality
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1oHnIb3GMNoKQ1CNXhQb7LIJ4vqEfv57B

item.title: Bus Or Tram
item.description:
The talk opens with a comic limerick and quickly turns it into a serious critique of fixed moral thinking: instead of asking what we ought to do, the speaker argues, people keep looking for prewritten rails for conduct. From there it moves through ethics, politics, and public fear, contrasting an experimental attitude with the anxious need to be right at all costs. Later, the talk widens into a complaint that modern life has lost any workable vision of what human beings might want, and it closes by attacking religious and political mindsets that prefer judgment and destruction to uncertainty.
Chapters: 00:00 Tramlines of Morality 02:25 The Dictionary Joke 04:30 Experiment Over Certainty 07:08 Politics Without Flexibility 10:31 A Missing Vision 13:30 What We Actually Want 16:50 Surface Design, Surface Living 19:45 Suicide and Judgment
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1EKeiT8BfJG57lYvpVhrZ0a8FSmeATlcL

item.title: Parallel Thoughts, East and West
item.description:
The talk opens by framing East and West as if they have been making parallel discoveries, using science, philosophy, psychology, and linguistics to show how similar ideas can arise independently. It then moves into a longer argument about bodily awareness and social conditioning, with a close look at Elsa Gindler’s work and Charlotte Selva’s accounts of learning to trust what the body actually senses. By the end, the speaker widens the point into a critique of rigid norms and a plea to work with nature rather than against it.
Chapters: 00:00 Parallel Beginnings 02:30 Science and Language 05:17 Psychology and Biology 07:58 Toward Taoist Practice 14:25 Elsa Gindler's Method 18:29 Labels and Conditioning 26:07 Listening Inward 30:36 Sanity with Nature
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=10vRX4mkGPMXlZi9vJQzBBzlQmN1YecW4

item.title: A Problem of Strategy
item.description:
The talk begins by rejecting the idea that military strategy is too technical for philosophy, then pivots quickly to the nuclear standoff between the United States and Russia. From there it argues, bluntly and at length, that atomic weapons are not only morally disastrous but strategically self-defeating, because they destroy the very control war is supposed to secure. The second half turns to alternatives: a non-lethal "peace gas" example, electronic disruption, and finally a judo-like strategy of yielding at the top level while organizing civil disobedience, sabotage, and guerrilla resistance below.
Chapters: 00:01 Philosophy Enters Strategy 01:37 The Nuclear Deadlock 03:08 War's Real Objective 06:00 Deterrence Is Not Control 08:07 Toward Useful Weapons 11:44 Wells and New Tactics 16:55 King Hall and Judo 20:13 Civil Resistance Strategy
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1zhGOD6OBbiO82J4B8Tjn87xEGlmUfGTn

item.title: The Useless in Art
item.description:
The talk opens with a complaint about grant applications and quickly turns into a defense of contemplation, art, and theater against the demand that everything justify itself by utility. From Genesis to Ionesco, the speaker argues that art is strongest when it is gratuitous, imaginative, and free of propaganda, and he uses examples from primitive decoration, representational painting, and even a Wall Street Journal art-investment ad to show how easily art gets folded back into survival and profit. The final movement broadens the claim into a meditation on nature: a tree, a cloud, or a work of art should not be reduced to what it is for, but encountered as something that simply is.
Chapters: 00:00 The Demand for Use 01:26 Genesis Recast 02:33 Ionesco on Art 05:14 Freedom and Humor 09:35 The Artist Emerges 13:40 Anti-Art and Reaction 16:54 Nature as Measure 20:13 Seeing Without Purpose 23:54 Play Against Profit
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1PBlpo0jQosOCtdEUZdnZHoaU5IIx8hOo

item.title: The Bhagavad Gita: The Song of the Lord
item.description:
The talk opens with Gandhi’s praise of the Bhagavad-Gita and uses it to frame a careful reading of the text as both spiritual classic and practical guide. It moves from the battlefield scene of Arjuna’s collapse into Krishna’s teaching on the indestructible self, then into larger ideas about Atman and Brahman, rebirth, duty, caste, and vocation. In its final stretch, the speaker returns to Gandhi and argues that the Gita’s central ethic is detached action: doing one’s work without fear, sentimentality, or attachment to results.
Chapters: 00:01 Gandhi Opens the Door 00:45 What the Gita Is 02:01 Arjuna on the Battlefield 04:08 The Indestructible Self 06:13 Atman and Brahman 11:16 Before Birth, After Death 13:56 Duty and Vocation 17:18 Gandhi and Nonviolence 20:04 Action Without Motive
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1R_YjBCrUnEdVCvLzMsNdlpIehSm60AwG

item.title: Paul Tillich on The Eternal Now
item.description:
The talk begins with a brief introduction to an experiment in sharing other philosophers’ words, then settles into a sustained reading from Paul Tillich’s "The Eternal Now." From there it moves through mortality and the fear of endings, the temptation to imagine endless future instead of eternity, and the Christian claim that the eternal stands above past and future. The later sections turn to memory, guilt, repentance, forgiveness, and finally the present as the place where time can be held and rest can be found.
Chapters: 00:01 Why this reading 01:50 The Eternal Now begins 03:46 Judging time from eternity 04:50 Past, present, future 07:17 Denial and endless time 10:12 Christian time and eternity 14:18 The burden of the past 18:08 Blessing, curse, forgiveness 20:45 The problem of presence
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1ik13ggisOTJBVcavHRid26OKs51UoQtQ

item.title: Discussion of Buddhist Mysticism
item.description:
The talk begins with a mention of D. T. Suzuki’s newly published book on Christian and Buddhist mysticism, then quickly turns to a sustained account of Shin, or Pure Land, Buddhism and its contrast with self-powered spiritual effort. From there, the speaker develops the idea of tariki through the vow of Amitabha and argues that what looks like passivity can also be read as a deeper release from anxious self-improvement. The second half shifts into a string of Ryokan anecdotes—playful, comic, and oddly moving—that put the teaching into lived form, ending with a portrait of a person who is at ease without making an effort to seem so.
Chapters: 00:00 Suzuki as springboard 00:35 The easy way 02:10 Amitabha’s vow 07:45 Against self-power 11:28 The Myokonin character 15:59 Zen and futility 17:12 Ryokan in anecdotes 24:48 Possessions and humor 29:55 The final turn
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1zdw-BNA2FvPSkZrqsPapxTyGLkvvifwo

item.title: Fundamentals of Buddhism
item.description:
The talk opens by correcting a previous point about Hinduism and Shankara, then uses that correction to frame a transition into Buddhism. From there it moves through the Buddha as an “awakened one,” the Wheel of Life, rebirth as a pattern of changing identity, and the Four Noble Truths as a medical-style diagnosis and cure. The final stretch brings the argument together around the Middle Way, nirvana as waking up rather than escape, and the Eightfold Path as a practical discipline of understanding, conduct, and meditation.
Chapters: 00:45 Correcting Shankara 05:26 Who the Buddha Is 07:54 Wheel of Life 10:13 Rebirth as Process 14:05 The Four Truths 17:52 The Middle Way 20:58 Grasping and Ignorance 23:40 Nirvana and the Path
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1UCyHBC_EQQlhzX_t4DFeugunc9B9nEQD

item.title: Fundamentals of Eastern Philosophy
item.description:
The talk returns to Hindu philosophy through Vedanta and spends most of its time correcting a common mistake: that liberation means fleeing the world. It moves from the idea of the self as the actor behind social roles, through a reading of maya as a mental distortion rather than the senses or the universe itself, and then turns to the Bhagavad Gita to argue for action without inner entanglement. By the end, the speaker broadens that claim into a larger affirmation of life, using the Puranas and temple imagery to show Hinduism as a tradition of participation rather than withdrawal.
Chapters: 00:00 Returning to Vedanta 00:54 God, self, and masks 03:47 Liberation from convention 05:49 The limits of social identity 10:41 Maya reconsidered 14:26 The Gita on action 17:18 Into the world again 22:02 Affirmation, not escape
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1J6rzzLsAovu8KcCwpPgNpTI88sGoASQ3

item.title: The Work of Sokei-an Sasaki
item.description:
The talk begins with the claim that Zen is suddenly fashionable in the United States, then shifts to a more personal introduction to Sokei-an Sasaki as an early Zen presence in New York and a formative influence on the speaker. From there it moves through a series of excerpts that show Sasaki at his most direct and disarming: a comic note on spontaneity, a long argument that Buddhism brings "no benefit" in any ordinary sense, and a first-person account of sudden awakening. The final stretch turns toward what Buddha means in this frame, the value of quiet contemplation, and a closing Bodhidharma story that ends on silence and sudden enlightenment.
Chapters: 00:01 Zen Boomlet 01:35 Meeting Sokei-an 03:52 Character and Color 06:03 Spontaneity and Nature 08:00 Studying Buddhism 15:17 No Benefit, No Profit 19:03 Satori and Buddha Mind 24:32 The Quiet Life 27:14 Bodhidharma's Silence
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1eG_x3jXrxWhUcLCA6DfhOS5N9B4tj1HI

item.title: Interview with Dr. Chang Chung-yuan
item.description:
The talk begins with a Chinese poem about a hermit who cannot be found, then moves into a wide-ranging interview on Taoism, non-interference, and the difference between forcing things and working with their nature. From there it turns to stories about the bottle ghost and other practical examples before shifting into denser ground: li and shi, appearance and reality, and the claim that Taoist wisdom is less about technique than about the kind of person who uses it. By the end, the conversation widens into an account of insight, interrelated selfhood, and a self-balancing universe named as Tao.
Chapters: 00:01 The Hidden Sage 02:23 Artificiality and Nature 05:00 Wu-wei in Practice 09:50 The Bottle Story 12:47 Science and Principle 16:15 Reality and Appearance 20:18 River, Judo, Cooperation 24:46 Insight Beyond Ego 26:15 The Self-Balancing Whole
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1NFjzLIGQEd3__bD_Zh-kHDh6DAEfMH1g

item.title: Spiritual Odyssey of Aldous Huxley
item.description:
This talk traces Aldous Huxley’s movement from witty, cynical novelist to spiritual writer, with the shift dated to the late 1930s and linked to Vedanta and Oriental philosophy. It pauses over criticism that he had merely swapped one negativity for another, then turns to Island as the book where his thinking comes into its fullest form. The discussion treats the novel less as fiction than as a philosophical and social tract, moving through its utopian island, altered family structure, drug use, education, and tantric practice before landing on the deeper idea of reconciling spirit and ordinary life.
Chapters: 00:01 Huxley’s turn 01:21 The critical dispute 03:41 From emptiness to inclusion 05:30 Island as counterweight 07:59 The utopian system 10:39 Why the book provokes 14:13 Not-two 17:00 Words and sorrow 19:01 Spirit and everyday life
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1c3YtY-392qlKdCYn55fgtw2iu307vm2i

item.title: Return to the Forest
item.description:
The talk begins with Joseph Campbell’s work on mythology and then uses his paper on “The Symbol Without Meaning” to trace how religious worldviews form, hold communities together, and eventually break apart. From there it moves through the mandala, caste, and the contrast between hunting and agrarian cultures, before turning to the modern condition: a fragmented world, chronic anxiety, and the search for a more direct, first-hand relation to experience. It ends by questioning authority itself, from priests and psychiatrists to Zen masters, and argues that what matters most cannot be handed down as doctrine. The thread throughout is a return to the forest as a figure for self-discovery outside inherited social patterns.
Chapters: 00:00 Campbell's Frame 02:40 Hunting and Shamanism 05:19 Agrarian Order 09:52 The Mandala Breaks 12:04 Return to the Forest 15:00 Beyond Social Patterns 17:56 Description and Anxiety 21:15 Finding It For Oneself 25:33 Alone Together
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1qCvh-AXuUsZBE5uALN5BNDAPZVo7e7ty

item.title: Eastern Orthodox Spirituality
item.description:
This talk opens as a postscript to a series on Christian philosophy, then turns into a sustained review of a book on Eastern Orthodoxy and what sets it apart from Western Christianity. The argument moves from broad contrasts — body and spirit, doctrine and mystery, individual and community — into the Orthodox ideas of theosis, kenosis, and a cosmos transfigured by divine love. From there it widens further, drawing careful parallels with Pure Land Buddhism, and closes with ascetic stories of despair, holy foolishness, and the odd freedom of surrendering personal will.
Chapters: 00:00 A Book on Orthodoxy 02:01 East and West Contrasted 03:59 Theosis and Cosmic Salvation 06:41 Kenosis and Creation 09:50 Other-Power Buddhism 12:46 Prelest and the Unfree Will 17:39 Despair Is the Danger 21:58 Evil, Shadow, and Resistance 24:58 Providence and Holy Fools
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1Ex9b8u9s3h0Q4b-jcG6Te9HhsGI43ZSs

item.title: The Future of Christian Philosophy VI
item.description:
This final installment of the series turns from the question of Christianity’s future to a sharper critique of how churches try to survive. The talk moves through fundraising, propaganda, and “defending the faith,” arguing that these habits reveal insecurity rather than conviction. From there it deepens into a case for faith as relinquishment: the grain that must die, the warning not to cling, and the claim that Christ belongs in the present rather than being locked in the past. It closes by recasting religion as the healing of a split between sacred and secular life, and of a deeper split in modern consciousness.
Chapters: 00:00 Why return to the topic 01:52 Church as administration 04:10 Attraction and overreach 07:01 Defending the faith 09:01 Faith versus clinging 13:41 Death, resurrection, and release 18:26 Dead rock, living flesh 24:36 Wholeness in daily life 28:05 Healing the split
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1tj_NQuO4BA-U0YVKjQLxXzawVS7rYgqs

item.title: The Future of Christian Philosophy V
item.description:
The talk continues a critique of Christian ideas of freedom by setting them against psychology, especially the way an isolated ego turns unconscious motives, guilt, and responsibility into a tangled problem. From there, it moves through liberation traditions, a reading of St. Paul, and a close look at confession and forgiveness, before arguing that the real issue is not moral effort but the false sense of being a separate self. It ends by pressing for a deeper transformation of how human identity is felt, not just how it is described.
Chapters: 00:00 Freedom and psychology 04:31 Liberation or license 06:02 The split self 08:52 The lonely ego 11:36 Christ lives in me 15:12 Church as one body 18:36 Grace, guilt, confession 23:18 Augustine and suspicion 27:21 Beyond absolute opposites
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1qnqpoHAOcTaCL5Up1lWAOW_pJddwPlp3

item.title: The Future of Christian Philosophy IV
item.description:
The talk revisits an earlier question about whether intelligence is best understood as pattern, then presses the contrast between mechanical order and organic growth. From there it turns to the human being as both object and interior experience, using Teilhard de Chardin to sharpen that split, before moving into the Christian problem of freedom, responsibility, sin, and the limits of a punitive view of God. It ends by asking whether Christian freedom can be kept from collapsing either into mechanistic determinism or into a juridical theology that makes freedom feel unreal.
Chapters: 00:00 A listener’s objection 00:54 Pattern versus mechanism 03:56 Nature grows from within 07:30 God as total pattern 08:48 The human image 13:03 Outside and inside views 15:34 Responsibility under pressure 19:56 Judgment and freedom
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1cY3egTMcYC5qDkzCDeVXs0Fs8qqeBg3u

item.title: Bang or Whimper Sequel
item.description:
The talk begins with a listener’s objection to an earlier claim about the earth ending “with a bang or a whimper,” and turns that challenge into a wider argument about human survival, technology, and the danger of taking the human race as the measure of everything. From there, the speaker moves through Taoist and comic examples—a banquet story, a fable about the body’s parts, and a joke about a farmer and the Lord—to press the case that humans are only one part of a larger field of life, not its crown. The final stretch widens into a meditation on relativity, certainty, and the unease people feel when there is no single central point of view.
Chapters: 00:01 The Survival Question 01:00 A Listener Objects 07:48 Taoist Counterpoint 10:04 Head and Stomach 13:06 The Human Weed 14:08 Fish Against Humanity 20:00 No Top Creature 24:29 Relativity and Release
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1YSsqWkxjBMIkAnHIakM0ogUdnkW80D9d

item.title: Bang or Whimper
item.description:
The talk begins with alarm over world population and quickly widens into a question about whether technological progress really serves human survival. From there it moves through Taoist warnings about interference, a reading of Norman O. Brown on repression and civilization, and a series of examples that test the value of stability against intensity. A plant stripped by aphids, a Freud quotation about a long miserable life, and the closing image of going out “with a bang or with a whimper” give the argument its shape: the obsession with survival may itself be what spoils life. It ends by suggesting that the deeper problem is not the end of the world, but our inability to live without fearing it.
Chapters: 00:00 Population Alarm 01:57 The Pace of Change 05:56 Taoist Restraint 08:57 Civilization and Repression 12:30 Bang or Whimper 15:00 Temperament and Survival 19:01 The Future as Excuse 22:22 Living Now 26:01 Handling Time
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1HejU9bSZz1mtk1zJg7mZydacL8JCQdaU

item.title: The Art of Psychoanalysis Part 3
item.description:
The talk opens by revisiting earlier remarks on psychoanalysis and a critique of J. Haley’s idea of therapy as a kind of one-upmanship, then quickly broadens into a defense of emotional openness. From there it moves through tenderness, embarrassment, and the limits of a cold scientific attitude toward sex, medicine, and psychotherapy, arguing that real healing depends on love as much as insight. In its later stretch, the speaker turns to trust, risk, Christianity, and Jung, before ending with a Taoist note that softness and suppleness belong to life, while hardness belongs to death.
Chapters: 00:00 One-Upmanship in Analysis 02:45 Feeling Beyond Control 05:32 Tenderness Repressed 10:37 Against Clinical Distance 13:58 The Patient Must Be Felt 17:04 Trust as a Gamble 21:49 Love, God, and Theology 26:03 Softness as Strength
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1p1dKdLpg7f8387BNG9Cea0oxieY2zKqC

item.title: The Art of Psychoanalysis Part 2
item.description:
The talk returns to J. Haley’s article on psychoanalysis and uses it as a frame for a larger comparison with Zen training. It moves from the idea of therapy as a kind of strategic trick or placebo, to the claim that real change often begins when the demand to be “one up” collapses. From there, the speaker develops the parallel with Zen barriers, self-consciousness, and the futility of trying to force sincerity, before widening the argument to the social and communicative context behind psychiatric illness.
Chapters: 00:00 Haley’s frame 02:13 One-upmanship and trickery 05:12 Placebo logic 07:36 Zen as inversion 09:50 Koans and surrender 13:13 The split self 18:30 Doubt reaches its limit 22:04 Acceptance, not control 24:16 The social matrix
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1jTGtNuPwf2DSaTkpm4lyaBvyU31MxrVh

item.title: The Art of Psychoanalysis Part 1
item.description:
The talk opens by framing psychoanalysis through a satirical article by J. Haley, then quickly turns that frame into a running joke about one-upmanship, silence, doubt, and the hidden status games between analyst and patient. From there it widens into the structure of the analytic setting itself—the couch, the fee, the analyst behind the patient, the use of silence, and the various ploys that keep the balance tilted. It ends by pulling back to the professional culture around psychoanalysis, where even meetings and technical language become contests for advantage, and by asking whether Haley is mocking the field or praising it in disguise.
Chapters: 00:00 Introducing Haley's Article 02:43 One-Upmanship Defined 06:25 Freud's Setting 10:54 Analyst and Patient Ploys 18:39 Limits and Variations 24:39 Professional One-Upmanship 26:57 Typical Course of Treatment 30:02 Final Question
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1WU-QwDD8ol1GKKSAxmj-BwHz3375DMsr

item.title: Consciousness and Concentration
item.description:
The talk begins by setting modern psychology alongside Freud, Gardner Murphy, and Hindu and Chinese thought, using those references to question whether the ordinary self is as solid or final as it seems. From there it turns to concentration: first as a problem of forcing the mind, then as something more natural, illustrated through Zen, Buddhist practice, and the image of trying to put legs on a snake. In its later movement, the talk widens into a critique of strain and heroic effort, then ends with a personal tribute to Lewis Hill and a direct plea to support KPFA.
Chapters: 00:00 Psychology and the larger self 02:41 The twist of perception 03:56 Why shortcuts matter 07:11 Laziness and simplification 10:44 Objects of concentration 15:32 The snake with legs 18:03 Against egoic strain 22:21 Effort, sensitivity, and character 25:05 Lewis Hill and KPFA
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1qN9s2jy3kybIfqCjx_-lSnRzoG7es2Ws

item.title: Observations on the Beat Way of Life
item.description:
The talk opens with a satirical letter mocking Bay Area bohemian life, then turns to the question of why the beat way of life provokes such strong hostility. From there, the speaker separates serious lives of vocation from shallow imitation, using anecdotes from Greenwich Village and a fishing-minded woman to argue that people should follow their real bent rather than the demands of status or productivity. The final movement broadens into a critique of the work ethic in an age of abundance, ending with the claim that much of the outrage around beat culture is really a fear of leisure, inner freedom, and the unexplored life beneath social roles.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening satire 01:02 Why the hostility 03:16 Respectable outrage 04:47 Following your bent 10:17 Imitation and caricature 14:05 Threat to the self-image 19:25 Production and leisure 23:56 The inner frontier
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1Wrid0hkY3Hb45hDOyq1x5pnipTC807hN

item.title: Insight and Ecstasy
item.description:
The talk opens from a KPFA book review of Philip Lamantia and Kenneth Rexroth’s comments on ecstatic poetry, then uses that exchange to separate ecstasy from genuine mystical insight. From there it moves through Eastern religion, a defense of Aldous Huxley as an intelligent witness to altered states, and into a longer argument that Buddhist awakening is really clarity about how thought manufactures false problems. In its later stretches, the talk turns more analytical, drawing on Benjamin Lee Whorf and Wittgenstein to question the way language creates the illusion of a separate doer behind action, thought, and perception.
Chapters: 00:00 Rexroth and Lamantia 02:40 Ecstasy and drugs 05:09 Insight first 07:44 Mystical language 09:50 Buddhist awakening 12:50 Thought makes problems 18:39 The ghost of the doer 23:59 Unity and release
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1HbB6xImrOvwwmROWPCnceu8bSHTzXFMt

item.title: Intro to Way Beyond the West
item.description:
This opening talk from the Way Beyond the West series explains what the speaker means by "way" or Tao, using Asian traditions to question the Western split between work, play, religion, philosophy, and science. The argument moves from a close reading of vocation and divine play to a sharper contrast between Western institutions and the more experiential, liberating aim he sees in Buddhism, Vedanta, and Taoism. It ends by shifting the emphasis from future plans to present awareness, and by saying the point is not conversion but a meeting of ideas between East and West.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the title 00:50 Way as Tao 02:59 Vocation and craft 05:11 Play instead of drudgery 08:03 Beyond creed and code 14:20 Community and convention 18:18 Theory and science 23:33 The living present 27:42 No conversion project
Source: KPFA Archive
Original page: https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1ie_caNNKSbFfVfGGQP2Iv5Bo93g_-OSP
